---
title: "Research Synergy & Technical Roadmap"
subtitle: "Proposed Mentorship: UPenn CHIBE Trainee Program"
author: 
  - name: "Yichao Jin, Ph.D."
    url: https://yichao2022.github.io
    affiliation: "The University of Texas at Dallas"
date: "2026-04-22"
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
    css: styles.css
    self-contained: true
---

## Executive Summary: The Research Bridge

My doctoral research at the University of Texas at Dallas focused on **Intertemporal Choice in Vaccination Timing**, specifically how waiting times and temporal discounting shape preventive health behaviors. By integrating Discrete Choice Experiments (DCE) with structural econometric models, I quantified the psychological barriers to vaccine uptake.

The synergy with **Dr. Alison Buttenheim's** work at **UPenn CHIBE** lies in the transition from *understanding* timing barriers to *designing* behavioral incentives that overcome them. My work provides the empirical foundation to optimize the **Behavioral Design** of nudges, ensuring that interventions are not only effective but also scalable across diverse populations.

## Methodological Toolkit

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
### Integrating Behavioral Economics with Computational Science
I employ a tri-pillar approach to behavioral intervention design:
:::

| Method | Application in My Research | Transferability to CHIBE |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Discrete Choice Experiments (DCE)** | Identifying latent preferences for vaccine attributes and temporal tradeoffs. | Optimizing incentive structures (e.g., financial rewards vs. convenience) for vaccine uptake. |
| **Structural Modeling** | Estimating causal parameters of decision-making under uncertainty. | Predicting the long-term impact of behavioral nudges on population-level health equity. |
| **Agent-Based Modeling (ABM)** | Simulating the diffusion of preventive behaviors in social networks. | Stress-testing scaling strategies for "nudges" before large-scale field implementation. |

## The "Digital Sandbox": TRIBE v2 Compute Core

A significant technical edge I bring to the Buttenheim Lab is the **TRIBE v2 (Technical Research Interface for Behavioral Economics)** compute core. 

### Why TRIBE v2?
- **High-Performance Simulation**: Optimized for Apple Silicon (M-series), allowing for complex ABM and Monte Carlo simulations on local hardware.
- **Data Privacy & Security**: At an institution like UPenn, handling sensitive patient data is paramount. TRIBE v2 ensures that all inference and model training occur **locally**, eliminating cloud dependency and ensuring strict compliance with HIPAA/IRB requirements.
- **Rapid Prototyping**: The "Digital Sandbox" allows us to "pre-test" behavioral incentives in a simulated environment, identifying potential failures or unintended consequences before committing to expensive field trials.

```{mermaid}
graph LR
    A[Empirical DCE Data] --> B{Structural Model}
    B --> C[Parameter Estimation]
    C --> D[TRIBE v2 Digital Sandbox]
    D --> E[Simulation of Incentives]
    E --> F{Optimal Nudge Selection}
    F --> G[Field Implementation]
    G --> A
```

## Proposed Synergy: Accelerating the Buttenheim Lab

If granted the opportunity to work under Dr. Buttenheim's mentorship, I aim to contribute to the lab's mission in the following ways:

*   **Incentive Optimization**: Applying structural choice models to refine the design of financial and social incentives for vaccine uptake in underserved communities.
*   **Scalability Audits**: Utilizing ABM to simulate how behavioral interventions scale across different demographic and geographic contexts, identifying "tipping points" for population-level behavior change.
*   **Grant & Publication Support**: My background in computational behavioral science allows for the rapid generation of simulation data and visual proofs-of-concept, which can strengthen grant applications (R01/RF1) and accelerate the publication cycle.
*   **Behavioral Digital Twins**: Developing "Digital Twins" of specific target populations to allow for real-time adjustments to behavioral designs as new field data arrives.

---

### Contact Information
[**Yichao Jin, Ph.D.**](https://yichao2022.github.io)  
Email: [Yichao.Jin@UTDallas.edu](mailto:Yichao.Jin@UTDallas.edu) | [jyc20220303@gmail.com](mailto:jyc20220303@gmail.com)  
Phone: +1 (682) 266-4185  
[GitHub](https://github.com/yichao2022) | [Website](https://yichao2022.github.io)
